headline: Mixed-Race Malaysians: 420,000 People Without a Policy Category
context: An estimated 420,000 mixed-race Malaysians — 1.3% of the population — fall between racial categories in a policy system that assumes everyone belongs to exactly one group.

cards.sub: Government forms offer single-race categories only. Mixed-race individuals must choose one parent's identity and erase the other.

cards.big: MyKad racial classification follows the father's ethnicity.
cards.sub: A Chinese-Malay child is classified as one or the other — never both. The policy denies half their identity.

cards.big: Mixed-race individuals with non-Bumiputera fathers lose Bumiputera benefits.
cards.sub: A child of a Kadazan mother and Chinese father is not Bumiputera despite 50% indigenous heritage.

cards.big: Brazil, the US, and New Zealand all allow multiple-race identification.
cards.sub: Malaysia's single-category system is increasingly outdated as intermarriage grows.

cards.big: A system that forces people to deny half their heritage is not classifying identity. It is amputating it.
Allowing multiple-race identification on MyKad would harm no one and validate 420,000 people. It is the simplest identity reform available — requiring a checkbox, not a constitutional amendment.
